title: Tomorrow
content:
I.
HER, that yer Honour was spakinï¿½ to? Whin, yer Honour? last yearï¿½
Standinï¿½ here be the bridge, when last yer Honour was here?
Anï¿½ yer Honour ye gev her the top of the morninï¿½, ï¿½Tomorraï¿½ says she.
What did they call her, yer Honour? They callï¿½d her Molly Magee.
Anï¿½ yer Honourï¿½s the thrue ould blood that always manes to be kind,
But thereï¿½s rason in all things, yer Honour, for Molly was out of her mind.

II.
Shure, anï¿½ meself remimbers wan night cominï¿½ down be the sthrame,
Anï¿½ it seems to me now like a bit of yisther-day in a dhrameï¿½
Here where yer Honour seen herï¿½there was but a slip of a moon,
But I hard thimï¿½Molly Magee wid her batchelor, Danny Oï¿½Roonï¿½
ï¿½Youï¿½ve been takinï¿½ a dhrop oï¿½ the crathurï¿½ anï¿½ Danny says ï¿½Troth, anï¿½ I been
Dhrinkinï¿½ yer health wid Shamus Oï¿½Shea at Kattyï¿½s shebeen;1
But I must be lavinï¿½ ye soon.ï¿½ ï¿½Ochone are ye goinï¿½ away?ï¿½
ï¿½Goinï¿½ to cut the Sassenach whateï¿½ he says ï¿½over the sayï¿½ï¿½
ï¿½Anï¿½ whin will ye meet me agin?ï¿½ anï¿½ I hard him ï¿½Molly asthore,
Iï¿½ll meet you agin tomorra,ï¿½ says he, ï¿½be the chapel-door.ï¿½
ï¿½Anï¿½ whin arc ye goinï¿½ to lave me?ï¿½ ï¿½Oï¿½ Monday morninï¿½ï¿½ says he;
ï¿½Anï¿½ shore thin yeï¿½ll meet me tomorra?ï¿½ ï¿½Tomorra, tomorra, Machree!ï¿½
Thin Mollyï¿½s ould mother, yer Honour, that had no likinï¿½ for Dan,
Callï¿½d from her cabin anï¿½ tould her to come away from the man,
Anï¿½ Molly Magee kern flyinï¿½ acrass me, as light as a lark,
Auï¿½ Dan stood there for a minute, anï¿½ thin wint into the dark.
But wirrah! the storm that nightï¿½the tundher, anï¿½ rain that fell,
Anï¿½ the sthrames runninï¿½ down at the back oï¿½ the glin ï¿½ud ï¿½a dhrownded Hell.

III.
But airth was at pace nixt morninï¿½, anï¿½ Hiven in its glory smiled,
As the Holy Mother oï¿½ Glory that smiles at her sleepinï¿½ childï¿½
Ethenï¿½she stept an the chapel-green, anï¿½ she turnï¿½d herself rounï¿½
Wid a diamond dhrop in her eye, for Danny was not to be founï¿½,
Anï¿½ manyï¿½s the time that I watchï¿½d her at mass lettinï¿½ down the tear,
For the Divil a Danny was there, yet Honour, for forty year.

IV.
Och, Molly Magee, wid the red oï¿½ the rose anï¿½ the white oï¿½ the May,
Anï¿½ yer hair as black as the night, anï¿½ yer eyes as bright as the day
Achora, yer laste little whishper was sweet as the lilt of a bird!
Acushla, ye set me heart batinï¿½ to music wid ivery word!
Anï¿½ sorra the Queen wid her sceptre in sich an illigant hanï¿½,
Anï¿½ the fall of yer foot in the dance was as light as snow an the lanï¿½,
Anï¿½ the sun kem out of a cloud whiniver ye walkt in the shtreet,
Anï¿½ Shamus Oï¿½Shea was yer shadda, anï¿½ laid himself undher yer feet,
Anï¿½ I loved ye meself wid a heart and a half, me darlinï¿½, and he
ï¿½Ud ï¿½a shot his own sowl dead for a kiss of ye, Molly Magee.

V.
But shure we wor betther frinds whin I crackï¿½d his skull for her sake.
Anï¿½ he ped me back wid the best he could give at ould Donovanï¿½s wakeï¿½
For the boys wor about her agin whin Dan didnï¿½t come to the fore,
Anï¿½ Shamus along wid the rest, but she put thim all to the door.
Anï¿½, afther, I thried her meself av the bird ï¿½ud come to me call,
But Molly, begorrah, ï¿½ud listhen to naither at all, at all.

VI.
Anï¿½ her nabours an frinds ï¿½ud consowl anï¿½ condowl wid her, airly and late,
ï¿½Your Danny,ï¿½ they says, ï¿½niver crasst over say to the Sassenach whate;
Heï¿½s gone to the States, aroon, anï¿½ heï¿½s married another wife,
Anï¿½ yeï¿½ll niver set eyes an the face of the thraithur agin in life
Anï¿½ to dhrame of a married man, death alive, is a mortial sin.ï¿½
But Molly says ï¿½Iï¿½d his hand-promise, anï¿½ shure heï¿½ll meet me agin.ï¿½

VII.
Anï¿½ afther her paï¿½rints had interï¿½d glory, anï¿½ both in wan day,
She began to spake to herself, the crathur, anï¿½ whishper, anï¿½ say
ï¿½Tomorra, Tomorra!ï¿½ anï¿½ Father Molowny he tuk her in hanï¿½,
ï¿½Molly, youï¿½re maninï¿½,ï¿½ he says, ï¿½me dear, av I undherstanï¿½,
That yeï¿½ll meet your paï¿½rints agin anï¿½ yer Danny Oï¿½Roon afore God
Wid his blessed Marthyrs anï¿½ Saints;ï¿½ anï¿½ she gev him a frindly nod,
ï¿½Tomorra, Tomorra,ï¿½ she says, anï¿½ she didnï¿½t intind to desave,
But her wits wor dead, anï¿½ her hair was as white as the snow an a grave.

VIII.
Arrah now, here last month they wor digginï¿½ the bog, anï¿½ they founï¿½
Dhrownded in black bog-wather a corp lyinï¿½ undher grounï¿½.

IX.
Yer Honourï¿½s own agint, he says to me wanst, at Kattyï¿½s shebeen,
ï¿½The Divil take all the black lanï¿½, for a blessinï¿½ ï¿½ud come wid the green!ï¿½
Anï¿½ where ï¿½ud the poor man, thin, cut his bit oï¿½ turf for the fire?
But och! bad scran to the bogs whin they swallies the man intire!
Anï¿½ sorra the bog thatï¿½s in Hiven wid all the light anï¿½ the glow,
Anï¿½ thereï¿½s hate enough, shure, widout thim in the Divilï¿½s kitchen below.

X.
Thim ould blind nagers in Agypt, I hard his Riverence say,
Could keep their haithen kings in the flesh for the Jidgemint day,
Anï¿½, faix, be the piper oï¿½ Moses, they kep the cat anï¿½ the dog,
But it ï¿½ud ï¿½a been aisier work av they lived be an Irish bog.

XI.
How-an-iver they laid this body they founï¿½ an the grass
Be the chapel-door, anï¿½ the people ï¿½ud see it that wint in to massï¿½
But a frish gineration had riz, anï¿½ most of the ould was few,
Anï¿½ I didnï¿½t know him meself, anï¿½ none of the parish knew.

XII.
But Molly kem limpinï¿½ up wid her stick, she was lamed iv a knee,
Thin a slip of a gossoon callï¿½d, ï¿½Div ye know him, Molly Magee?ï¿½
Anï¿½ she stood up strait as the Queen of the worldï¿½she lifted her headï¿½
ï¿½He said he would meet me tomorra!ï¿½ anï¿½ dhropt down dead an the dead.

XIII.
Och, Molly, we thought, machree, ye would start back agin into life,
Whin we laid yez, aich be aich, at yet wake like husbanï¿½ anï¿½ wife.
Sorra the dhry eye thin but was wet for the frinds that was gone!
Sorra the silent throat but we hard it cryinï¿½ ï¿½Ochone!ï¿½
Anï¿½ Shamus Oï¿½Shea that has now ten childer, hansome anï¿½ tall,
Him anï¿½ his childer wor keeninï¿½ as if he had lost thim all.

XIV.
Thin his Riverence buried thim both in wan grave be the dead boor-tree,2
The young man Danny Oï¿½Roon wid his ould woman, Molly Magee.

XV.
May all the flowers oï¿½ Jeroosilim blossom anï¿½ spring from the grass,
Imbrashinï¿½ anï¿½ kissinï¿½ aich otherï¿½as ye didï¿½over yer Crass!
Anï¿½ the lark fly out oï¿½ the flowers wid his song to the Sun anï¿½ the Moon,
Anï¿½ tell thin in Hiven about Molly Magee anï¿½ her Danny Oï¿½Roon,
Till Holy St. Pether gets up wid his kays anï¿½ opens the gate!
Anï¿½ shure, be the Crass, thatï¿½s betther nor cuttinï¿½ the Sassenach whate
To be there wid the Blessed Mother, anï¿½ Saints anï¿½ Marthyrs galore,
Anï¿½ singinï¿½ yer ï¿½Avesï¿½ anï¿½ ï¿½Pathersï¿½ for iver anï¿½ ivermore.

XVI.
Auï¿½ now that I tould yer Honour what-iver I hard anï¿½ seen,
Yer Honour ï¿½ill give me a thrifle to dhrink yer health in potheen.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
